section.title: Nebuchadnezzar's Madness and Restoration
section.sectionRationale: Told as the king's own testimony, this chapter traces the humbling of the proudest man on earth. Nebuchadnezzar dreams of a towering tree cut down to a stump, Daniel warns him to repent, and a year later, boasting on his palace roof, he is driven mad to live like a beast until he acknowledges that the Most High rules. Restored at last, he praises the King of heaven. It is the great biblical parable of pride abased and grace given.
section.thesis: The Most High rules over the kingdom of mankind and gives it to whom He wishes, so that even the greatest king, when he boasts in his own glory, is brought low to the level of the beasts until he lifts his eyes to heaven and confesses that God alone reigns and is able to humble the proud.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the king's opening confession of praise; the dream of the great tree cut to a stump; Daniel's interpretation and call to repent; the sentence fulfilled in seven years of madness; and the king restored, humbled, and praising the King of heaven.

units.label: The king's opening confession
units.note: his kingdom is an eternal kingdom

verses.text: King Nebuchadnezzar, To the people of every nation and language who dwell in all the earth: May your prosperity be multiplied.
I am pleased to declare the signs and wonders that the Most High God has performed for me.
How great are His signs, how mighty His wonders! His kingdom is an eternal kingdom; His dominion endures from generation to generation.

units.label: The dream of the great tree
units.note: cut it down, but leave the stump

verses.text: I, Nebuchadnezzar, was at ease in my house and flourishing in my palace.
I had a dream, and it frightened me; while I was in my bed, the images and visions in my mind alarmed me.
So I issued a decree that all the wise men of Babylon be brought before me to interpret the dream for me.
When the magicians, enchanters, astrologers, and diviners came in, I told them the dream, but they could not interpret it for me.
But at last, into my presence came Daniel (whose name is Belteshazzar after the name of my god, and in whom is the spirit of the holy gods). And I told him the dream:
“O Belteshazzar, chief of the magicians, I know that the spirit of the holy gods is in you and that no mystery baffles you. So explain to me the visions I saw in my dream, and their interpretation.
In these visions of my mind as I was lying in bed, I saw this come to pass: There was a tree in the midst of the land, and its height was great.
The tree grew large and strong; its top reached the sky, and it was visible to the ends of the earth.

Its leaves were beautiful, its fruit was abundant, and upon it was food for all. Under it the beasts of the field found shelter, in its branches the birds of the air nested, and from it every creature was fed.
As I lay on my bed, I also saw in the visions of my mind a {{ir|watcher}}, a holy one, coming down from heaven.
He called out in a loud voice: ‘Cut down the tree and chop off its branches; strip off its leaves and scatter its fruit. Let the beasts flee from under it, and the birds from its branches.

But leave the stump with its roots in the ground, with a band of iron and bronze around it, in the tender grass of the field. Let him be drenched with the dew of heaven and graze with the beasts on the grass of the earth.
Let his mind be changed from that of a man, and let him be given the mind of a beast till {{iddan|seven times}} pass him by.

This decision is the decree of the watchers, the verdict declared by the holy ones, so that the living will know that the {{illaya|Most High}} rules over the kingdom of mankind and gives it to whom He wishes, setting over it the lowliest of men.’

This is the dream that I, King Nebuchadnezzar, saw. Now, Belteshazzar, tell me the interpretation, because none of the wise men of my kingdom can interpret it for me. But you are able, because the spirit of the holy gods is in you.”

units.label: Daniel interprets and calls him to repent
units.note: you, O king, are that tree

verses.text: For a time, Daniel, who was also known as Belteshazzar, was perplexed, and his thoughts alarmed him. So the king said, “Belteshazzar, do not let the dream or its interpretation alarm you.” “My lord,” replied Belteshazzar, “may the dream apply to those who hate you, and its interpretation to your enemies!
The tree you saw that grew large and strong, whose top reached the sky and was visible to all the earth,
whose foliage was beautiful and whose fruit was abundant, providing food for all, under which the beasts of the field lived, and in whose branches the birds of the air nested,
you, O king, are that tree! For you have become great and strong; your greatness has grown to reach the sky, and your dominion extends to the ends of the earth.

And you, O king, saw a watcher, a holy one, coming down from heaven and saying: ‘Cut down the tree and destroy it, but leave the stump with its roots in the ground, with a band of iron and bronze around it, in the tender grass of the field. Let him be drenched with the dew of heaven, and graze with the beasts of the field till seven times pass him by.’
This is the interpretation, O king, and this is the decree that the Most High has issued against my lord the king:

You will be driven away from mankind, and your dwelling will be with the beasts of the field. You will feed on grass like an ox and be drenched with the dew of heaven, and seven times shall pass you by, until you acknowledge that the Most High rules over the kingdom of mankind and gives it to whom He wishes.
As for the command to leave the stump of the tree with its roots, your kingdom will be restored to you as soon as you acknowledge that Heaven rules.

Therefore, may my advice be pleasing to you, O king. Break away from your sins by doing what is right, and from your iniquities by showing mercy to the oppressed. Perhaps there will be an extension of your prosperity.”

units.label: The sentence fulfilled in madness
units.note: driven out to live like a beast

verses.text: All this happened to King Nebuchadnezzar.
Twelve months later, as he was walking on the roof of the royal palace of Babylon,
the king exclaimed, “Is this not Babylon the Great, which I myself have built as a royal residence by the might of my power and for the glory of my majesty?”
While the words were still in the king's mouth, a voice came from heaven: “It is decreed to you, King Nebuchadnezzar, that the kingdom has departed from you.

You will be driven away from mankind to live with the beasts of the field, and you will feed on grass like an ox. And seven times will pass you by, until you acknowledge that the Most High rules over the kingdom of mankind and gives it to whom He wishes.”

At that moment the sentence against Nebuchadnezzar was fulfilled. He was driven away from mankind. He ate grass like an ox, and his body was drenched with the dew of heaven, until his hair grew like the feathers of an eagle and his nails like the claws of a bird.

units.label: Restored and humbled, he praises heaven
units.note: able to humble those who walk in pride

verses.text: But at the end of those days I, Nebuchadnezzar, looked up to heaven, and my sanity was restored to me. Then I praised the Most High, and I honored and glorified Him who lives forever: “For His dominion is an everlasting dominion, and His kingdom endures from generation to generation.

All the peoples of the earth are counted as nothing, and He does as He pleases with the army of heaven and the peoples of the earth. There is no one who can restrain His hand or say to Him, ‘What have You done?’”

At the same time my sanity was restored, my honor and splendor returned to me for the glory of my kingdom. My advisers and nobles sought me out, and I was restored to my throne, and surpassing greatness was added to me.
Now I, Nebuchadnezzar, praise and exalt and glorify the King of heaven, for all His works are true and all His ways are just. And He is able to humble those who walk in {{gevah|pride}}.

ir.gloss: A watcher, a holy one, a heavenly being who observes and carries out God's decree. The word may also be rendered an angelic watcher or messenger. In the dream the watcher announces the verdict of heaven against the king, showing that the sentence comes from God's court, not from chance.

iddan.gloss: A set time or period. Seven iddan, seven times, are appointed to pass over the humbled king; most take this as seven years. The fixed span shows that even the king's judgment and restoration run on God's timetable, not the king's.

illaya.gloss: The Most High, the exalted one above all. The refrain of the chapter is that the Most High rules over the kingdom of mankind and gives it to whom He wishes. The word settles the whole contest: the highest king on earth must learn that there is One higher, who sets up and brings down rulers as He pleases.

gevah.gloss: Pride, haughtiness, self-exaltation. The king's boast, by the might of my power and for the glory of my majesty, is the very gevah God brings low. The chapter ends with Nebuchadnezzar's own confession that God is able to humble those who walk in pride, the moral of his whole ordeal.

basic.context: This chapter is written as Nebuchadnezzar's public testimony. He dreams of a huge tree, sheltering all creatures, that a heavenly watcher orders cut down to a stump. Daniel gently tells him the tree is the king himself, and warns him to repent by doing right and showing mercy. A year later, boasting on his rooftop about the Babylon he built, the king is struck with madness and lives like an animal for seven periods, until he looks up to heaven, his sanity returns, and he praises the God who rules over all.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter is a testimony of pride humbled and grace given, framed by the king's own praise. The stump left in the ground is a sign of mercy: judgment is real but not final. The turning point is when Nebuchadnezzar lifts his eyes to heaven, a picture of true repentance. Christians differ over whether the king was fully converted, but the text clearly means us to learn the danger of pride and the certainty that God alone reigns.
basic.originalLanguages: The key word is illaya, the Most High, whose rule the king must acknowledge. A heavenly ir, a watcher, decrees the sentence; the humbling lasts seven iddan, seven appointed times. And the whole story is a lesson against gevah, pride, which God is able to bring low.
basic.typology: The tree cut down to a stump, then made to flourish again, echoes the pattern of humbling and exaltation that runs through Scripture and reaches its center in Christ, who was brought low and then raised. The proud king abased and then restored is a warning and an invitation that anticipates the gospel call to humble ourselves under God's mighty hand.
basic.christ: Where Nebuchadnezzar was humbled for his pride, Jesus humbled Himself willingly, taking the form of a servant, and God exalted Him. The chapter's truth, that God resists the proud and gives grace to the humble, is the very logic of the cross. Christ is the King of heaven whom Nebuchadnezzar finally praises, whose dominion truly is everlasting.
basic.god: God is the Most High who rules over every kingdom and gives it to whom He wishes. He is sovereign over the proudest ruler, patient in giving a year to repent, just in the judgment, and merciful in the restoration. No one can restrain His hand or call Him to account, yet He delights to lift up the humble.
basic.crossReferences: Proverbs 16 warns that pride goes before destruction. Isaiah 14 pictures the fall of a proud king. And James 4 and 1 Peter 5 teach that God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble, the lesson Nebuchadnezzar learns the hard way.
basic.oneStory: The mightiest king of the age is brought to the ground and then raised again when he acknowledges the Most High. His story preaches to every empire and every heart: God alone reigns, pride will be humbled, and grace is given to those who look up to heaven. It points ahead to Christ, the humble King whom God exalted, before whom every knee will bow.

context.literary: Uniquely, most of the chapter is a first-person royal proclamation, framing the narrative of humbling with hymns of praise at the start and finish. It is the third of the court narratives and the high point of the Nebuchadnezzar cycle, which began with his dream in chapter 2 and his confession in chapter 3.
context.historical: Ancient kings issued proclamations of the great deeds of their gods; here that form is turned to praise the God of Israel. Babylon the Great was famous for its walls, gates, and hanging gardens, the very glory the king boasts in. A period of the king's incapacity is plausibly reflected in the historical record of his reign, though the details are debated.
context.canonical: The chapter belongs to the Bible's long teaching on pride and humility, from the tower of Babel to the fall of the king of Tyre and Babylon in the prophets. Its refrain that the Most High rules the kingdom of mankind anticipates the New Testament proclamation that all authority belongs to the exalted Christ, Matthew 28:18.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To testify that the Most High rules over every kingdom and humbles the proud, using the greatest king of the age as the object lesson, and to move both rulers and readers to acknowledge God, repent of pride, and praise the King of heaven.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes God's dealing with one particular king; it does not promise the same madness for every proud person. What it prescribes, through Daniel's counsel and the king's confession, is repentance, mercy to the oppressed, and humble acknowledgment that God alone reigns.

debates.question: Was Nebuchadnezzar genuinely converted?

views.label: Yes, a true, if limited, conversion
views.case: His closing words praise, exalt, and glorify the King of heaven, confess God's justice, and acknowledge his own pride; this reads like real repentance and worship, and God restores him, suggesting genuine humbling of heart.

views.label: A real acknowledgment, but uncertain saving faith
views.case: He confesses God's supremacy and even calls Him the Most High, yet still speaks of the spirit of the holy gods and my god; the text records his submission without clearly settling the state of his soul, so we should hold the question with humility.

debates.bearing: Either way the chapter's aim is clear: it summons every proud heart to acknowledge that God alone rules and to seek His mercy, and it leaves the final judgment of any soul to God.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's drumbeat is illaya, the Most High, the exalted God whose rule over the kingdom of mankind the king must learn. A heavenly ir, a watcher or holy messenger, pronounces the sentence, so that the verdict clearly comes from God's court. The humbling is measured out in seven iddan, seven appointed times, on God's calendar and not the king's. And the whole ordeal is aimed at gevah, pride: the boast made on the rooftop is the pride God brings crashing down, until the king himself confesses that God is able to humble those who walk in pride.

typology.type: The great tree cut to a stump, then restored
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of humbling and exaltation fulfilled in Christ, brought low in death and raised in glory, Philippians 2:8-9.

typology.type: The proud king abased and then lifted up
typology.fulfillment: The gospel principle that God resists the proud but exalts the humble, 1 Peter 5:5-6, ultimately in Christ.

typology.type: The King of heaven whose dominion is everlasting
typology.fulfillment: The risen Christ, given all authority in heaven and on earth, whose kingdom will never end, Matthew 28:18 and Daniel 7:14.

study.christ: Nebuchadnezzar was humbled against his will for his pride; Jesus humbled Himself willingly, taking the very nature of a servant, and therefore God exalted Him to the highest place. The chapter's deep truth, that God brings low the proud and lifts up the lowly, is the logic of the cross and resurrection. The King of heaven whom Nebuchadnezzar finally praises, whose dominion is everlasting and whose works are all true and just, is fully revealed in Jesus, before whom every knee in heaven and on earth will one day bow.
study.god: God is the Most High, ruling over the kingdom of mankind and giving it to whom He wishes; the peoples of the earth are as nothing before Him, and no one can restrain His hand or call Him to account. Yet He is not a tyrant: He warns the king a full year ahead, leaves a stump of mercy in the ground, and restores the humbled ruler who looks to heaven. He is the sovereign who opposes pride and gives grace to the humble.

crossReferences.note: Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall, the very pattern of the king's rooftop boast.
The fall of the proud king who said he would ascend above the heights, echoing Nebuchadnezzar's humbling.
God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble; humble yourselves and He will lift you up.
All authority in heaven and on earth given to the risen Christ, the King whose dominion is everlasting.

study.oneStory: The greatest king of the age is brought down to graze like an ox and then raised again the moment he lifts his eyes to heaven and confesses that the Most High rules. His testimony preaches to every empire and every proud heart: God alone reigns, pride will surely be humbled, and grace is given to those who look up. The chapter points ahead to the King of heaven, Jesus, who humbled Himself and was exalted, and before whom every ruler and every knee will finally bow.

quiz.q: What did the great tree in the king's dream represent?

options.text: Nebuchadnezzar himself, grown great and strong, whose greatness would be cut down.
options.note: Daniel 4:22 says plainly, you, O king, are that tree.

options.text: The kingdom of God growing to fill the earth.
options.note: That is the stone of chapter 2; here the tree is the king, soon to be humbled.

options.text: The nation of Israel in exile.
options.note: The tree is explicitly the king, not Israel.

options.text: The garden of Eden restored.
options.note: The tree pictures Nebuchadnezzar's greatness, not Eden.

quiz.q: What counsel did Daniel give the king before the judgment fell?

options.text: Break from your sins by doing right and showing mercy to the oppressed, that your prosperity might continue.
options.note: Daniel 4:27 records his call to repentance and mercy.

options.text: Offer more sacrifices to the Babylonian gods.
options.note: Daniel called him to righteousness and mercy, not to idol worship.

options.text: Flee the city before the dream came true.
options.note: Daniel urged repentance, not escape.

options.text: Ignore the dream as meaningless.
options.note: Daniel took the dream seriously and urged the king to change.

quiz.q: What triggered the fulfillment of the judgment?

options.text: The king's proud boast on the palace roof, crediting his own power and glory for Babylon's greatness.
options.note: Daniel 4:30-31 shows the sentence fell as he boasted.

options.text: An attack by a foreign army.
options.note: No army; it was his pride that brought the decreed judgment.

options.text: A plot by his own advisers.
options.note: The judgment came from heaven in response to his pride, not a plot.

options.text: A famine in the land.
options.note: It was his boasting, not a famine, that triggered the sentence.

quiz.q: How did the king's ordeal end?

options.text: He looked up to heaven, his sanity returned, and he praised the Most High as the true and everlasting King.
options.note: Daniel 4:34-37 records his restoration and praise.

options.text: He died in his madness, never recovering.
options.note: He was restored, not left in madness; the stump signaled mercy.

options.text: He was overthrown and replaced by Daniel.
options.note: He was restored to his own throne after acknowledging God.

options.text: He remained proud to the very end.
options.note: He ended by praising and glorifying the King of heaven.

quiz.q: What is the chapter's central lesson about God?

options.text: The Most High rules over the kingdom of mankind and is able to humble those who walk in pride.
options.note: This refrain and the king's closing words state the theme, Daniel 4:17, 37.

options.text: God rewards kings who build great cities.
options.note: The opposite; the king's boast in his building brought judgment.

options.text: God stays out of the affairs of nations.
options.note: The chapter insists God rules over every kingdom of mankind.

options.text: God can be manipulated by powerful rulers.
options.note: No one can restrain His hand or call Him to account.

questions: Nebuchadnezzar boasted, look what I have built by my own power. Where are you tempted to take credit for what is really a gift from God?
God gave the king a full year to repent before judgment fell. How does God's patience toward you call you to change while there is still time?
The turning point came when the king looked up to heaven. What would it look like for you to lift your eyes from your own achievements to God?
The stump was left in the ground as a sign of mercy. Where do you need to trust that God's discipline in your life is not the end of the story but an invitation back?
God gives grace to the humble but opposes the proud. Where is pride quietly at work in your heart, and how might you humble yourself before God lifts you up?

translationNotes.note: Watcher may also be rendered an angelic watcher or a messenger; the same figure appears in verses 17 and 23 as the herald of heaven's decree.
Seven times most likely means seven years, the appointed span of the king's humbling; the phrase stresses God's set timetable.
Heaven rules is a reverent way of saying God rules; the leftover stump signals that the kingdom will be restored when the king acknowledges this.
The king's condition, living and eating like an ox, is often identified with a form of madness; the point is his reduction from the height of pride to the level of a beast.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Daniel 4:1-37. The em dash at the end of verse 21 was converted to a comma. Poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse. Chapter 4 is Aramaic, so load-bearing lexicon entries use Aramaic. No em or en dashes used.
